posts.name: Benjamin Hand
posts.cooked:
I have been very happy with the direction of red Bordeaux over the past 5+ years. It seems that through a combination of improved practices in the vineyard and changes in the cellar, many chateaux have begun to produce red Bordeaux (at least on the Left Bank) that is fresher, more aromatic, less extracted, and slightly lower in alcohol. To me it represents a lovely synthesis of classic claret married with the realities of a warmer climate.

My question is whether there is any sign that producers have begun to rethink white Bordeaux in the same way? I love traditional white Bordeaux – long-lived, textured, with a heavy percentage of semillon. Instead, many chateaux have taken the opposite approach to compete with Sancerre and New Zealand – Sav Blanc dominated with Sancerre clones. There are still some producers that seemingly have resisted this trend (DDC, Bouscaut, L-HB, etc), but they are a dwindling few.

Any hope that there will be a movement to marry a classic rendition of white Bordeaux with the modern climate?

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
I’m a big fan of white Bordeaux. I love the big, Semillon-influenced Classifieds from Pessac-Leognan but also love basic Graves, E2M, white Bordeaux and Bordeaux Sauvignon Blanc as everyday drinkers.

For the most part, I don’t think these less expensive white Bordeaux are trying to compete with Loire and NZ, I think they are tweaking a wonderful style that has existed for a long time. I find notes of hay and grass that that I don’t often get from the Loire, without the grapefruit aromas and flavors that ruin NZ versions for me.

From your note, it sounds like the less expensive white Bordeaux are not part of your mix. Please let us know. Graville-Lacoste has been a go-to for me for decades, I also like Clos Floridene (more money, harder to find) and Chantegrive.

link_counts.title: Region Vintage Orientation in 4 Bottles for 2025

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked: Following…

posts.name: Benjamin Hand
posts.cooked:
I guess I am less concerned with the direction of less expensive white Bordeaux, and more interested in the philosophy of “serious” White Bordeaux producers in Pessac and elsewhere in Bordeaux. Serious doesn’t have to mean expensive, as illustrated by Bouscaut, Larrivet Haut Brion, etc. But rather estates trying to make white Bordeaux that rivals other top white wines around the world. It seems to me that the influence of Denis Dubourdieu has been towards a more sauvignon blanc focused interpretation of white Bordeaux, even at the high end. See, for example, white Bordeaux projects like Le Petit Cheval and Les Champs Libres (recognizing these are Right Bank estates).

I guess my concern is that old-school white Bordeaux, dominated by semillon and long-lived, seems increasingly unfashionable and my hope is that there will be a counter-revolution that recognizes that you can make crisp, flinty SB blanc other places, but that Bordeaux has the opportunity to produce age-worthy white wines that stand in good company with other top white wines from around the world. Perhaps there is some hope that estates in Barsac and Sauternes will begin to allocate some of their best semillon and SB vines to dry white Bordeaux as fewer sweet wines are sold?

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
I’m quite interested in the topic. Thanks for starting it.

Are ageworthy BB producers switching to DIAM? I know premox was a problem aging BB in the past.

You didn’t encounter it or hear about it very often but that was because most of us just drink so few BB — as a percentage I think it used to be as bad as Burgundy and other ageable dry whites.

posts.name: Benjamin Hand
posts.cooked: Great question. I know that Domaine de Chevalier switched to Diam in 2015 for their whites. I haven’t heard of other producers making the switch, but I would strongly support that. I have also had premox issues.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I think this argument is difficult to sustain, given that the vast majority of white Bordeaux producers adopted Dubourdieu’s thiol-driven Sauvignon-dominant approach to white wine wholesale, giving the same passionfruit, grapefruit and boxwood vegetation aromas you find in Sauvignon all over the world and above all NZ.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I would look at old Laville Haut-Brion (1982 and before), and plenty of other producers’ old wines come to that (old Carbonnieux, for example, was Sémillon-dominant and can be magnificent). Today, Couhins (not to be confused with Couhin-Lurton) is Sauvignon-dominant but not in the estery, thiol-driven style and a really nice wine, from a cool clay-limestone terroir. The whites made by the Guinadeau family of Lafleur are very solid, too, again in a Sauvignon-driven style (but one that nods to Sancerre more than NZ).

For a Sémillon-driven style, however, you should look at the C de Sec from Château Closiot, made by Jean-Marie Guffens (of Burgundy fame) and the most recent vintages of Lune d’Or from Clos des Lunes, which Olivier Bernard of Domaine de Chevalier is behind.

BTW, your analysis of the state-of-the-art is spot on, and the subject of one of the many articles I haven’t had time to write…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Many have, including Domaine Clarence Dillon. Generally, in the first years, there is a bit of a learning curve whereby sulfite levels are a bit high for the much more reductive closure, and the first vintages can be a bit tight/reduced out of the gates as a result (as often happens when producers transition in Burgundy and elsewhere).

posts.name: Owen Jones
posts.cooked: How do you categorize current Carbonnieux in terms of these trends we are trying to quantify? I’ve only had the 2021 recently, and while I loved it, it seems to be a somewhat polarizing style of bordeaux blanc amongst some individuals I’ve spoken to, which surprised me.

posts.name: Jim Stewart
posts.cooked: IMG_3070851×699 192 KB

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
William,

Thank you for your comment. I admit to being a little puzzled and would be grateful for any elucidation.

I believe that all of the wines I mentioned have significant proportions of Semillon, which to me sets them apart from the pure Sauvignons you mention. Half a century ago white Graves was a staple for me. Do many of them now seem to have a little more bright Sauvignon character? Yes, but to me it is nicely offset by the Semillon. Also, while I get the passionfruit and especially the boxwood, I don’t get grapefruit at all; if I did, I wouldn’t still be drinking them!

posts.name: Jim Freeman
posts.cooked: 2021 Carbonnieux blanc is totally delicious right now.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
The biggest issue with White Bordeaux today is climate change. In the very hot, dry vintages the wines can lack acidity. In the cooler years, there are numerous wines from Pessac Leognan, and the Medoc which can be outstanding.

While they are not the savior of White Bordeaux, I really enjoy several of the new blends you find in the Medoc and Right Bank with non Bordeaux varietals.

Chardonnay, Gros Manseng, Viognier, Petit Manseng, Albarino and Savagnin are grapes I see in blends with Sauvignon and Semillon today.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
As a casual consumer I have never understood the value proposition of white Bordeaux. $100+, let alone $200 or more, is a whole lot for a white wine and I’ve never had a white Bordeaux that seemed striking or distinctive enough to set it apart from other regions in the way even lower level white Burgundy can do. I’d rather explore Italy/Australia/NZ/Oregon/Loire for less money or you could also get the best satellite or village apellations in white Burgundy for similar money.

Compare red Bordeaux where the value proposition is actually fantastic, with age-worthy Cabernet blends at every price point that are far superior to what you can get elsewhere for the same $, or it’s even impossible to get the same combination of quality and ageability.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
As a casual consumer I have never understood the value proposition of red Bordeaux. $100+, let alone $200 or more, is a whole lot for a red wine and I’ve never had a red Bordeaux that seemed striking or distinctive enough to set it apart from other regions in the way even lower level red Burgundy can do.

Compare white Bordeaux where the value proposition is actually fantastic, with age-worthy Semillon blends at every price point that are far superior to what you can get elsewhere for the same $

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
William,

So you are saying many are now sauv Blanc driven rather than having a good amount of semillon?

And how are they achieving a thiol driven style? Picking decisions? Fermentation decisions?

Thanks for your input - always invaluable . . .

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Marcu_Stanley:

As a casual consumer I have never understood the value proposition of white Bordeaux. $100+, let alone $200 or more,

I do not know where you shop, but most Pessac Leognan are under $50. Domaine de Chevalier Blanc is under $100.

DDC Blanc is a world class wine IMO.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Dan_Kravitz:

Graville-Lacoste has been a go-to for me for decades

Yup. A total no-brainer.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Jeff_Leve:

The biggest issue with White Bordeaux today is climate change. In the very hot, dry vintages the wines can lack acidity. In the cooler years, there are numerous wines from Pessac Leognan, and the Medoc which can be outstanding.

What are some recent vintages that you think are especially good for white Bdx?

Edit: I looked on your site and found this comment

2021 Dry white Bordeaux wines are superb. In fact, this is the best vintage for white Bordeaux wine in years. The wines offer everything fans of Bordeaux Blanc could ever want in a wine. They exude purity, freshness, lift, and vibrancy. The fruits are aromatic, sweet, ripe, and sizzling with the racy acidities that are unique to Bordeaux. It is interesting to note that only one wine this year garnered a perfect 100 Pt score, and it is white wine!
Read more at:2021 Bordeaux Blanc Report, Tasting Notes, Vintage Info, Ratings

posts.name: Steve Costigan
posts.cooked: Ch de Fieuzal blanc with about eight years on it is often my sweet spot for white Bordeaux

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: It was your observation that “I don’t think these less expensive white Bordeaux are trying to compete with Loire and NZ” that I was taking issue with, rather than your particular examples. Clos Floridene, however, despite a healthy percentage of Sémillon, was one of Denis Dubourdieu’s own properties and the style very much reflected/reflects his approach to white wine vinification. To me, this is the by far the dominant aesthetic in Bordeaux white wine production today.

posts.name: Brian Pinci
posts.cooked: I’m partial to Malartic Lagraviere Blanc. The 2014 I had recently was delicious. I also opened recently the 2015 Pape Clement Blanc and the 2013/14 DDC Blancs - all were wonderful wines. I don’t care too much for the Malartic red but the white is special.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Cool fermentations, accompanied by use of specific enzymes (e.g. LALLZYME CUVÉE BLANC™ (100g) | Scott Laboratories) and yeasts (e.g. ZYMAFLORE® X5 - oenological yeast - White and rosé wines - LAFFORT®)…

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Chris_Seiber:

What are some recent vintages that you think are especially good for white Bdx?

Hey Chris… You already saw my comment on 2021. It’s about a good as a vintage can get in White Bordeaux. Other recent years that are solid across the board; 17, 14, and 13.

That doesn’t mean some producers didn’t make good or even very good whites in warm years, because they did. It was an overall look at vintages.

I forgot to mention that numerous estates in Sauternes are also working hard on making quality dry white Bordeaux.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Jeff_Leve:

I forgot to mention that numerous estates in Sauternes are also working hard on making quality dry white Bordeaux.

Any ones we should be looking out for? Especially ones that aren’t super expensive?

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Chris_Seiber:
Jeff_Leve:

I forgot to mention that numerous estates in Sauternes are also working hard on making quality dry white Bordeaux.

Any ones we should be looking out for? Especially ones that aren’t super expensive?

Only Ygrec, the dry white of Yquem is pricey. Everything is not much money.

Some of the dry whites from Sauternes I like a lot, **Lafaurie Peyraguey Grand Vin Sec, Suduiraut Vieille Vignes Grand Vin Blanc Sec, Suduiraut Pur Semillon, Doisy Daene Grand Vin Sec and Clos des Lunes from the Bernard family that own, and produced Domaine de Chevalier, to name a few.

Hope this helps …

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
Thank you for the clarification.

I certainly understand that there is a finite market for Sauvignon and in that sense white Bordeaux competes with Loire and NZ (and California and Chile and and and). In general I prefer moderately priced white Bordeaux to many Loire and other Sauvignons because the brash style you describe is ameliorated by the inclusion of Semillon.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Dan_Kravitz:

because the brash style you describe is ameliorated by the inclusion of Semillon.

If you claim this, have you tasted these more extreme examples at all? I’ve tasted some Bdx Blancs that have had some Semillon in the blend, yet the wines would’ve been easily confused for a NZSB had they been poured blind. In these kinds of wines, that Semillon component really does nothing.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
Can you please give me specific examples of wines to avoid?
Yes, if it’s modern-style Bdx Blanc with ~5% Semillon, of course it could mimic the NZ / Loire style. And even if it’s 20%+ of seriously overcropped Semillon, it would be lost. But the specific wines I mentioned all have proportions of Semillon that add significant subtlety.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
Request:

Outside of white Bordeaux, my go-to wine in this style has been Chaleur Estate Blanc. I find it has similarities to DDC Blanc.

Does anybody have recommendations of less expensive wines in a similar style?

Thank you.

posts.name: P@trick Lynch
posts.cooked: Nothing really to add except I had a terrific bottle of Chateau Talbot Calliou Blanc in March.

posts.name: John Pollard
posts.cooked: I challenge you to seek out a Chateau d’Yquem “Y” Ygrec with a few years on it. IMO, higher-end white BDX is mind blowing and underpriced.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked: I’m in Marcus Stanley’s camp; I’ve tried most of the top names and don’t care for them. If anything, the lesser lights are more in my preference zone. Some of white Graves that I like include Clos Floridene, and Ch. Ducasse (which is not the KLWM one; his is Bordeaux AOC). I feel like modern consumers aren’t going to cellar whites, which means the potential ageability & complexity of the better names doesn’t get harnessed. Additionally I feel like whites are thrown back more casually, and faster, than reds with less time to savor what ever interest the better ones offer.

fwiw I had Laville-HB 1999 and Haut-Brion 1999 side by side recently, both in a fantastic spot, Laville brighter, livelier, minerally and more acidity driven, HB fuller, exotic with peach, mango, egg yolk, mouthfilling, a matter of taste which you prefer.
However both pricey and no bargain white Bordeaux -
but at the very top of possible quality

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
10000041321920×2561 435 KB

Mentioned upthread, but the 2018 Ch. Ducasse ‘blanc’ [Graves] continues to delight and is keeping well. 13% abv, sealed under DIAM3, with a semillion heavy blend. It is savory, with a bitter citrus pith character, that tastes more appealing than my description. It’s also a bit fuller and fleshier than lower priced Bordeaux blanc. I like it and it was - for me - a rare repurchase after trying one. I have not seen it around before/after, so who knows what it’s US distribution is like. I’d give this an A- and will let my last bottle age another year.

If the future of white Bordeaux included more quality names like this - available on the shelf - the outlook would be good.

posts.name: Benjamin Hand
posts.cooked: The wines are certainly too young to show their full potential, but for anyone who wants to try a good comparison of White Bordeaux vs. a good domestic example, K&L current has the 2021 Larrivet H-B Blanc and the 2022 Delille Chaleur White.
